query: association between gut microbiome and depression

retrieved_docs.title: Psychological Issues Associated With Obesity -- Etiology -- Biological Etiologies
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors significantly influence the relationship between obesity and psychological well-being, with specific genes predisposing individuals to obesity itself as well as to related mental health conditions. [8] [9] This genetic predisposition underlies the interactions between physical health and mental health, where the balance of hormones regulating hunger, satiety, and stress, such as leptin, ghrelin, and those involved in the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, can be disrupted by obesity. [9] [10] Such hormonal imbalances may lead to altered cortisol levels, contributing to stress, anxiety, and depression. [10] Furthermore, obesity is associated with chronic low-grade inflammation, which has been linked to an increased risk of depression through its effects on brain function. [11] Insulin resistance, another consequence of obesity, affects cognitive changes and mood disorders, highlighting insulin's critical role in brain function and neurotransmitter signaling. [12] Research also suggests that obesity can alter brain structure and function, particularly in areas involved in emotion regulation, reward processing, and decision-making, potentially leading to psychological disorders. [13] The emerging understanding of the gut-brain axis points to how obesity-induced changes in the gut microbiota can influence neurotransmitter production and behavior. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Effects on Child Health(Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Biological Factors
retrieved_docs.content: The gut microbiome includes the trillions of microorganisms that inhabit the human gut. Alterations in the gut microbiome can lead to weight gain through numerous pathways. [15] The dominant gut florae are Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes (90%), Proteobacteria , Actinobacteria , and Fusobacteria . These bacteria have a symbiotic relationship with their host. They can be affected by various factors, such as gestational age at birth, premature rupture of membranes, mode of delivery of the infant, type of feeding, feeding practices, and antibiotics usage. The maturation of gut flora occurs from birth to adulthood and is determined by various genetic factors, diet, lifestyle, and environment. Gut microbiota helps maintain the mucosal barrier, nutrient digestion (especially the synthesis of short-chain fatty acids), and immune response against pathogens. The imbalance of the gut microbiome (dysbiosis), leading to increased production of short-chain fatty acids, has been linked to developing obesity and other medical conditions, such as type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, Metabolic syndrome, anxiety, and depression. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Nucleus Raphe -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The fact that other treatment modalities for depression target dopamine, norepinephrine, and catecholamine transmission implies that the serotonergic system is one of the multiple components in the pathogenesis of depression. For example, in patients who respond well to selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, the depletion of catecholamines rather than serotonin is more closely linked to symptomatology. Furthermore, some patients experience improvement of symptoms when SSRI therapy is augmented with a second agent such as a dopamine agonist, highlighting a multifactorial etiology. Current research explores novel targets to alleviate MDD symptoms and to do so more rapidly. [10] Also, the investigation into the microbiota-gut-brain axis holds promise for future treatment modalities. While the raphe nuclei synthesize the majority of serotonin in the central nervous system, most of the serotonin in the entire body gets synthesized in the gastrointestinal tract. Research linking abnormal gut microbiota to depressive symptoms highlights environmental factors in the pathogenesis of MDD. Studies demonstrating that depressive symptoms are reversible with transplantation of normal microbiota holds promise for future therapeutic measures. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Brain Gut Adipocyte Interaction -- Issues of Concern -- Causes of peripheral leptin resistance include:
retrieved_docs.content: Leptin resistance in peripheral organs, e.g., in the liver, heart, and skeletal muscles, are not well studied. According to some studies, leptin resistance in peripheral organs might be due to reduced activation of signaling protein or increased production of leptin signaling inhibitors. 4) Brain trauma 5) Brain surgery of craniopharyngioma 6) Inflammatory lesions of the hypothalamus 7) Psychological disorders, such as depression and anxiety 8) Sleep deprivation causes abnormal elevation of sympathetic outflow and overactivation of orexin neurons 9) Genetic mutations in receptors or hormones and neurochemicals. (Pro-opiomelanocortin gene mutation) 10) Microbial dysbiosis due to a high-fat diet 11) Decreased intestinal barrier functions 12) Inflammation of gut due to a high-fat diet 13) Metabolic endotoxemia 14) Chronic low-grade systemic inflammation 15) Desensitization of vagal afferent nerves

retrieved_docs.title: Psychiatric Complications of Bariatric Surgery -- Etiology -- Gut-Brain Axis
retrieved_docs.content: Alterations in the gut microbiome can impact mood and behavior by influencing gut peptides such as ghrelin, glucagon-like peptide 1, peptide YY, and cholecystokinin. These gut peptides have also been associated with increased rewarding effects of alcohol post-bariatric surgery. [23] Interestingly, the emergence of glucagon-like peptide 1 agonists in obesity treatment has led to modulations of reward pathways, offering potential avenues for addiction treatment, as suggested by Brown et al. [23] Furthermore, alcohol and other substance use have also been linked to gut dysbiosis, potentially creating a vicious cycle affecting mood and behavior.

retrieved_docs.title: Lifestyle Mental Wellbeing for the Primary Care Visit -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: There are 4 primary mechanisms by which diet notably affects psychological health: its impact on the gastrointestinal microbiome, inflammation, epigenetics, and macronutrients and micronutrients. The gastrointestinal microbiome produces substances (eg, GABA, dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine) that enter the brain through the bloodstream. Tryptophan, a precursor to serotonin, appears to have the most significant impact on mental health. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Functional Abdominal Pain in Children -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The development of functional abdominal pain is likely multifactorial. Some children appear to exhibit a heightened sensitivity to the range of normal physiological sensations, resulting in visceral hyperalgesia. For example, sensations such as bloating or indigestion may cause pain that exceeds what is typically experienced by unaffected individuals. In some cases, an initial sensitizing event, such as an infection, allergy, altered gut microbiome, or motility disorder, occurs first and later progresses to hypersensitivity, characterized by increased sensations of pain or other visceral sensations, including bloating or cramping. Psychosocial factors such as stress or comorbid anxiety and depression are also associated with the development of hypersensitivity. [2] [4] Some research results suggest that functional abdominal pain may develop in children with other painful disorders, such as Crohn disease. In these cases, visceral hypersensitivity may develop, especially in patients with coexisting anxiety. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Psychiatric Complications of Bariatric Surgery -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of psychiatric complications following bariatric surgery appears to involve a complex interplay of physiological, psychological, and nutritional factors. [23] Surgically induced modifications to the gastrointestinal tract can potentially disrupt the gut microbiome and alter normal absorption processes, which may affect the pharmacokinetics of alcohol, other substances, and various medications. [23] Such changes could conceivably lead to higher plasma concentrations of these substances, potentially amplifying their psychological effects and contributing to the onset or worsening of psychiatric symptoms.

retrieved_docs.content: 2) Obesity due to the above-mentioned causes should be addressed, and appropriate counseling and treatment should be prescribed (for example, leptin resistance should be treated with recombinant leptin).

retrieved_docs.title: Depression in Children -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of depression is multifactorial and complex, resulting from interactions between biological vulnerabilities and environmental factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Tyramine -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: TAAR1 has been found in large amounts in the stomach and small intestine and may play a role in the brain-gut-microbiome axis. [24] TAAR1 is the only subtype not represented in the olfactory epithelium; however, the utility of TAAR subtypes in olfaction is still being studied. [24] One study found an association between single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNPs) in the TAAR1 gene and fibromyalgia. [25] The peripheral vascular effects and resulting hypertension with tyramine ingestion, long attributed to norepinephrine displacement, may partly be the result of TAAR1 binding mechanisms as well. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Brain Gut Adipocyte Interaction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Obesity due to central or peripheral leptin resistance requires treatment with leptin based therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Brain Gut Adipocyte Interaction -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotic use in early life can lead to obesity in later part of life. Specific alteration in the composition and function of the human gut microbiome can affect weight. Obesity due to alteration in the gut microbiome can have treatment with prebiotics, probiotics, synbiotics, or even fecal transplant. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Irritable Bowel Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Gut-Directed Psychotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Gut-directed psychotherapy offers another valuable treatment option, particularly for patients with IBS linked to psychological stress or mood disorders. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and gut-directed hypnotherapy have demonstrated effectiveness in reducing the frequency and severity of symptoms, primarily in patients with mood-directed IBS symptoms. These interventions address the disrupted gut-brain axis believed to underlie IBS. Current guidelines do not recommend selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) or fecal microbiota transplants for IBS management, given insufficient evidence supporting their use in this context. [32] [33] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Lifestyle Mental Wellbeing for the Primary Care Visit -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Consuming healthy foods also positively impacts the diversity and composition of the microbiome via the production of the desirable effects of neuroendocrine pathways, with proven clinical improvement in patients with generalized anxiety disorder, schizophrenia, and bipolar disorder. In addition to eating adequate whole foods, diets should be low in processed foods, refined carbohydrates, sugars, and trans-saturated fats. It is hypothesized that highly processed foods are recognized as foreign invaders in the human body, consequently leading to inflammatory cascades (measured by the Dietary Inflammatory Index) that ultimately are detrimental to physical and mental health. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Stress Reaction -- Organ Systems Involved -- Gastrointestinal System
retrieved_docs.content: Stress-induced changes in gut motility can manifest as diarrhea or constipation, whereas increased visceral sensitivity may contribute to symptoms such as irritable bowel syndrome. In addition, stress impairs the integrity of the gastrointestinal mucosal barrier, leading to increased permeability and susceptibility to inflammation and infection. Dysregulation of the gut-brain axis, mediated by stress, exacerbates gastrointestinal disorders and can worsen symptoms through bidirectional communication between the central nervous system and the gut microbiota. [17] Furthermore, stress-related alterations in gut microbiota composition and diversity can further contribute to gastrointestinal dysfunction, highlighting the intricate interplay between stress and gastrointestinal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of اضطراب معدي معوي (gastrointestinal disorder). This gastrointestinal disorder is associated with مرض نفسي جسدي (psychosomatic disease), indicating that disturbances of the gut may have a significant psychophysical component. Similarly, ضيق النفس (shortness of breath) is also linked to مرض نفسي جسدي, highlighting that respiratory symptoms can be manifestations of psychosomatic processes. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of recognizing the interplay between gastrointestinal, respiratory, and psychosomatic factors in patient assessment and management.

Within the psychological domain, اضطراب نفسي serves as a parent category; اضطراب توحدي is a broader subgroup; اضطراب قلق الانفصال is a narrower subcategory; and اكتئاب is positioned as a child condition. All listed conditions are recognized as having identifiable etiological qualifiers, and each may give rise to specific complications: death, انسداد الحالب, فرط ضغط الدم الخبيث, نوبة, اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), lung disease caused by external agents, and complications of intestinal infection. A clinically important association is the link between اعتلال عصبي سكري and قرحات الطرفين السفليين, highlighting neuropathic damage as a driver of ulcer formation. Prevention and control measures are applicable across the entire spectrum, emphasizing proactive management in psychiatric, vascular, renal, metabolic, infectious,

retrieved_docs.content: is explicitly connected to follicular cyst, intestinal infection and the mechanisms underlying adult respiratory failure, depression, cystitis, autism spectrum disorder, separation‑anxiety disorder and choking. The pathophysiology of glandular disease and lip tumor is also noted, and prevention‑and‑control measures are associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, arrest, depression, cystitis, autism spectrum disorder, separation‑anxiety disorder, apnea, glandular disease and lip tumor. Apnea is specifically mapped to reduced blood oxygen levels, illustrating the interconnection between pathophysiological understanding, preventive strategies and diverse disorders. Collectively, genetic, cultural, mechanistic and preventive considerations intersect across hematologic, respiratory, infectious, neoplastic, urologic, gastrointestinal and neurodevelopmental/psychiatric conditions, guiding comprehensive assessment, diagnosis and integrated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Psychological disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad category of mental health conditions that encompasses more specific disorders such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation‑anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال), each of which is a narrower subtype within the overall class of psychological disorders. Both the general category of psychological disorder and its child conditions—depression and separation‑anxiety disorder—are identified as targets for prevention and control measures, indicating that strategies aimed at preventing onset, reducing severity, and managing symptoms are applicable across the spectrum of these mental health conditions. This hierarchical relationship underscores the importance of applying preventive and control interventions at both the overarching disorder level and the individual disorder level to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2292
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety disorder due to another medical condition, also described as anxiety disorder due to a known physiological condition, is a distinct psychiatric diagnosis that requires the presence of specific diagnostic criteria. This disorder is characterized by anxiety symptoms that are directly attributable to an underlying medical or physiological condition, and clinicians must identify the causal medical condition and apply the established criteria to confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما (plasma hyperosmolarity) is clinically associated with ارتفاع السكر في الدم (elevated blood glucose) and with حالة نفسية غير طبيعية (abnormal psychological state). The presence of plasma hyperosmolarity often signals severe hyperglycemia and can contribute to or reflect disturbances in mental status, underscoring the need to monitor both metabolic derangements and neuropsychiatric symptoms in patients presenting with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Mental disorder (اضطراب نفسي) is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses more specific conditions such as depression (اكتئاب) and separation anxiety disorder (اضطراب قلق الانفصال). Separation anxiety disorder is identified as a narrower form within the overall class of mental disorders, while depression is listed as a child concept of mental disorder, indicating its status as a distinct subtype. Epidemiology is recognized as an appropriate qualifier for mental disorder, depression, and separation anxiety disorder, underscoring the relevance of epidemiological research in describing the prevalence, distribution, and risk factors of these psychiatric conditions. This hierarchical organization and the emphasis on epidemiological data together support a comprehensive understanding of how these disorders are related and inform patient care and public‑health strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2769
retrieved_docs.content: Fatigue (التعب) is described as having anxiety (القلق) as a defining characteristic, indicating that feelings of anxiety are integral to the clinical presentation of fatigue. In addition, dietotherapy is identified as an allowed qualifier for both anxiety (القلق) and fatigue (التعب), meaning that nutritional or dietary interventions are recognized as permissible therapeutic options for managing these conditions. This interconnection highlights the relevance of considering both psychological symptoms and dietary strategies in the comprehensive care of patients experiencing fatigue and anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2844
retrieved_docs.content: The designation “Allowed qualifier” indicates that dietoterapie (dietary therapy) is recognized as an appropriate and permissible intervention for several distinct health conditions. Specifically, dietoterapie is considered an allowed therapeutic approach for اكتئاب (depression), for عدوى بفيروس معوي (enteric viral infection), and for gout and other crystal arthropathies. Incorporating dietary modifications into the management plans for these disorders can therefore be justified and may contribute to overall patient care.
